ios_development.content: iOS development uses Swift (primary) or Objective-C with Xcode IDE. SwiftUI is the modern declarative UI framework (iOS 13+) with state management via @State, @Binding, @ObservedObject, @Published, and @EnvironmentObject. UIKit remains relevant for complex custom UI and iOS 12- compatibility. Core Data provides object graph management and persistence with SQLite backend. Combine framework provides reactive streams (Swift alternative to RxSwift). App Store submission requires a paid Apple Developer Program account ($99/year), adherence to App Review Guidelines (2.3 - accurate metadata, 2.5 - software requirements, 4.0 - design, 5.1 - privacy, 5.2 - intellectual property), and use of Xcode Archive and Organizer for binary upload. TestFlight enables beta distribution with up to 10,000 external testers. In-app purchases use StoreKit framework with consumable, non-consumable, auto-renewable subscription, and non-renewing subscription product types. Key patterns: MVVM with SwiftUI + Combine, coordinator pattern for navigation, and dependency injection for testability.

android_development.content: Android development uses Kotlin (primary, Google endorsed) or Java with Android Studio. Jetpack Compose is the modern declarative UI toolkit (replaces XML layouts) with composable functions, state hoisting, and Material Design 3 theming. Room provides SQLite abstraction with compile-time query verification. WorkManager handles deferrable background tasks (guaranteed execution, battery-friendly). ViewModel stores UI state across configuration changes. Navigation Compose manages screen transitions with type-safe arguments. Play Store distribution requires a $25 one-time registration, adherence to Developer Program Policies (deceptive behavior, malware, user data, harmful content), and app signing by Google Play. In-app billing uses Play Billing Library. Material Design 3 (Material You) provides dynamic color theming based on wallpaper. Key patterns: repository pattern for data access, use cases for business logic, unidirectional data flow (UDF) with StateFlow, and dependency injection with Hilt.

react_native.content: React Native enables building mobile apps using JavaScript/TypeScript and React. The new architecture (Fabric renderer + TurboModules) improves performance and interoperability. Hermes is an open-source JavaScript engine optimized for mobile (small binary, fast startup, precompiled bytecode). Native Modules bridge JavaScript and native code (Java/Kotlin for Android, Objective-C/Swift for iOS) using TurboModules (lazy loading) and Fabric components (synchronous native rendering). Expo is the most popular framework for RN development, providing managed workflow (OTA updates, EAS Build, config plugins) and 200+ pre-built native modules. Navigation: React Navigation (stack, tab, drawer) with native stack navigator. Performance: FlatList for large lists (with getItemLayout), FastImage for optimized images, and react-native-reanimated for 60fps animations. State management: Redux Toolkit, Zustand, Jotai, or React Query for server state. Key tools: React Native Debugger, Flipper, and Firebase Crashlytics.

flutter.content: Flutter is Google's UI toolkit for building natively compiled multi-platform apps from a single Dart codebase. The framework renders its own widgets using Skia (Impeller on iOS) for consistent 120fps performance. Widget tree composability: StatelessWidget for immutable UI, StatefulWidget for mutable state. State management: Riverpod (compile-safe, testable, family providers for parameters), Bloc (event-driven with cubits), Provider (inherited widget wrapper), and GetX (minimal boilerplate). Platform channels enable native API access via MethodChannel (Dart -> native) and EventChannel (native -> Dart). Flutter web renders with CanvasKit (Skia WASM) or HTML renderer. Desktop: Windows (Win32/UWP), macOS (Cocoa), Linux (GTK). Key packages: Dio (HTTP), GoRouter (declarative routing), Hive/Isar (local storage), Freezed (immutable models), flutter_gen (assets). Build modes: debug (JIT, hot reload), profile (performance), release (AOT, optimized). CI/CD: Codemagic (Flutter-native), Fastlane, GitHub Actions.

desktop_windows.content: Windows desktop development offers multiple frameworks. WinUI 3 is the modern native UI framework (Windows App SDK) with Fluent Design, xaml-based layouts, Mica/acrylic materials, and WinRT APIs. WPF (.NET Framework/.NET Core) remains widely used in enterprise with XAML, data binding, MVVM, control templates, and third-party controls (Telerik, DevExpress). UWP is the modern (but declining) store-based framework with sandboxed execution and adaptive UI. .NET MAUI (.NET Multi-platform App UI) targets Windows alongside macOS, iOS, and Android from shared C#/XAML codebase. MSIX is the modern packaging format with identity, extension points, and automatic updates via Store or sideloading. Deployment: MSI installers (WiX Toolset), ClickOnce, App Installer, Store submission. Key patterns: MVVM with CommunityToolkit.Mvvm, dependency injection (Microsoft.Extensions.DependencyInjection), and async/await for responsive UI.

desktop_macos.content: macOS desktop development uses Swift with AppKit (traditional) or SwiftUI (modern). AppKit provides mature window management, responder chain, Core Animation layers, and Auto Layout. SwiftUI for macOS supports menus, keyboard shortcuts, toolbar customization, window grouping, and document-based apps. Mac Catalyst enables iPad apps to run on macOS with minimal changes. Sandboxing is enforced for Mac App Store distribution with entitlements for specific capabilities (network, file access, camera). Notarization is required for all macOS software (not just App Store) - submit to Apple for security scanning. DMG packaging provides disk image distribution with custom background artwork and Applications folder alias. Key patterns: MVC with AppKit, MVVM with SwiftUI, Combine for data binding, and Grand Central Dispatch for concurrency. Keychain Services for secure credential storage.

desktop_linux.content: Linux desktop development spans multiple toolkits and packaging formats. GTK (C, Python, Rust, Vala) is the GNOME toolkit with widget hierarchy, CSS theming, and accessibility support. Qt (C++, Python, Rust) is the cross-platform toolkit for KDE with QML for declarative UI, signals/slots, and extensive widget library. Electron (JS/TS) packages web apps with Chromium and Node.js for cross-platform desktop distribution. Flatpak provides sandboxed application distribution with runtimes, permissions, and centralized updates via Flathub. Snap (Canonical) offers auto-updating, confined packages with Snap Store. AppImage provides single-file portable applications. FUSE enables filesystems in userspace. D-Bus provides inter-process communication on the Linux desktop. Key patterns: MVVM with Qt/QML, GtkApplication for GTK, DBus activation for services. Desktop entries (.desktop files) for application launchers.

web_frontend.content: Web frontend development combines HTML5 semantic markup, CSS3 with Flexbox/Grid/Custom Properties, and JavaScript/TypeScript. Responsive design uses mobile-first approach with media queries, relative units (rem, em, vw, vh), and container queries. Accessibility (WCAG 2.1 AA minimum) requires semantic HTML, ARIA attributes when needed, keyboard navigation, sufficient color contrast, and screen reader testing. Performance optimization focuses on Core Web Vitals: Largest Contentful Paint (LCP < 2.5s), First Input Delay (FID < 100ms), Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS < 0.1). Techniques: code splitting, lazy loading (Intersection Observer), image optimization (WebP/AVIF, responsive srcset), font subsetting, critical CSS inlining, and CDN caching. Build tools: Vite, webpack, esbuild, Turbopack. CSS methodologies: BEM, CSS Modules, Tailwind CSS, CSS-in-JS. Testing: Lighthouse, Playwright, Cypress, Vitest.

web_backend.content: Web backend development provides server-side logic, data processing, and API services. RESTful APIs follow resource-oriented design (GET /users, POST /users, PUT /users/:id, DELETE /users/:id) with proper HTTP status codes, HATEOAS for discoverability, and versioning via URL or headers. GraphQL provides flexible queries with typed schema, resolver functions, DataLoader for batching, and persisted queries for optimization. gRPC uses protocol buffers for service definition with bidirectional streaming and HTTP/2 transport. WebSocket enables full-duplex communication for real-time features (chat, live updates, collaboration). Server-Sent Events (SSE) provides one-way real-time updates over HTTP. CQRS separates read and write operations into different models. Event sourcing stores state changes as an append-only event log for audit trail and temporal queries. Authentication: session-based (cookies), token-based (JWT), OAuth 2.0 / OIDC. Rate limiting: token bucket, leaky bucket, sliding window.

full_stack.content: Full-stack frameworks provide integrated frontend and backend solutions. Next.js (React) offers file-based routing (App Router), SSR, SSG, ISR, middleware, API routes, and React Server Components - with Vercel platform optimization. Remix (React) emphasizes web fundamentals with nested routes, loaders/actions, progressive enhancement, and forms. Nuxt (Vue) provides auto-imports, modules, SSR/SSG, server routes, and middleware - with Nitro engine. SvelteKit (Svelte) offers file-based routing, form actions, server-only modules, and adapter-based deployment. Blazor (.NET) enables C# in the browser via WebAssembly or server-side rendering with SignalR. JHipster generates full-stack Spring Boot + Angular/React/Vue applications with production-ready features (monitoring, CI/CD, security). Key considerations: data fetching strategy (SSR vs CSR vs SSG vs ISR), authentication integration, database access, deployment model, and team expertise. Choose based on rendering requirements, real-time needs, and ecosystem preferences.

microservices.content: Microservices architecture decomposes applications into independently deployable services. Service decomposition follows bounded contexts (DDD), single responsibility, and independent data stores. Inter-service communication: synchronous (HTTP/REST, gRPC) or asynchronous (events, message queues). Circuit breaker pattern (Resilience4J, Polly, Hystrix) prevents cascading failures with states: closed (normal), open (fail fast), half-open (test recovery). Service mesh (Istio, Linkerd, Consul) provides infrastructure layer for traffic management (routing, retries, timeouts), security (mTLS, authorization), and observability (metrics, tracing, access logs). API gateway (Kong, AWS API Gateway, Spring Cloud Gateway) handles cross-cutting concerns: authentication, rate limiting, routing, composition, and caching. Observability combines logging (structured, correlation IDs), metrics (RED metrics: Rate, Errors, Duration), and distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry, Jaeger, Zipkin). Deployment: Kubernetes, Docker Compose, serverless (AWS Lambda, Azure Functions).

serverless.content: Serverless computing abstracts infrastructure management, automatically scaling from zero. AWS Lambda supports Node.js, Python, Java, Go, .NET, Ruby, and custom runtimes (via Lambda Layers). Azure Functions offers similar capabilities with integrated bindings (input/output to Azure services). Cloudflare Workers runs on V8 isolates at the edge (fast cold starts, global distribution). Vercel Edge Functions deploy JavaScript/TypeScript to Vercel's Edge Network. Cold starts are a key concern: optimization strategies include keeping functions warm (provisioned concurrency), minimizing dependencies, using faster runtimes (Node.js vs Python), and SnapStart (Lambda, pre-initialized snapshots). Billing models charge per request and compute duration (GB-seconds). Best practices: stateless design (use external storage), idempotent handlers (retries are common), proper error handling (dead letter queues), timeout management (Lambda max 15 min), and monitoring with CloudWatch/DataDog. Framework: Serverless Framework, AWS SAM, Terraform, Pulumi.

api_design.content: API design principles ensure usable, maintainable interfaces. Richardson Maturity Model levels: Level 0 (plain XML/JSON over HTTP), Level 1 (resources), Level 2 (HTTP verbs), Level 3 (HATEOAS - hypermedia controls). OpenAPI/Swagger provides machine-readable API specifications with schema validation, code generation, and interactive documentation. API versioning strategies: URL path (/v1/users), header (Accept: application/vnd.api+json;version=1), or query parameter (?version=1). Rate limiting algorithms: token bucket (burst allowance), leaky bucket (smooth rate), sliding window (accurate time window). Pagination: cursor-based (recommended for real-time data, stable references), offset-based (simple, but unstable with inserts). HATEOAS includes links in responses for discoverability (self, next, prev, related). Error responses should use consistent format (RFC 7807 Problem Details): type, title, status, detail, instance. Idempotency keys prevent duplicate processing for POST requests.

database_design.content: Database design decisions affect performance, scalability, and maintainability. Schema design follows normalization (eliminate redundancy through 3NF/BCNF) for transactional workloads, or denormalization for read-heavy/analytical workloads. Indexing strategies: B-tree indexes for equality and range queries, covering indexes (include all needed columns), partial indexes (WHERE clause), composite indexes (column order matters - match query filter order). Partitioning splits large tables into smaller pieces: range (date ranges), list (categories), hash (even distribution). Sharding distributes data across servers: horizontal sharding (same schema, different rows), vertical sharding (different tables per shard). Key considerations: choose shard key to avoid cross-shard queries, maintain data consistency, handle resharding. Connection pooling (PgBouncer, HikariCP, SQLAlchemy pool) manages database connections efficiently. Migration tools: Alembic (Python), Flyway (Java), Prisma Migrate (JS/TS), Goose (Go). Backup strategies: full, incremental, point-in-time recovery (WAL archiving for PostgreSQL).

authentication.content: Authentication verifies identity. OAuth 2.0 is the industry delegation framework with grant types: Authorization Code (most secure, with PKCE for mobile/SPA), Client Credentials (server-to-server), Implicit (deprecated, use PKCE), and Device Authorization (CLI/smart TVs). OpenID Connect (OIDC) adds identity layer on OAuth 2.0 with ID Token (JWT containing user claims) and UserInfo endpoint. SAML 2.0 uses XML assertions for enterprise SSO (common in Microsoft/ADFS, Okta, OneLogin). JWT (JSON Web Token) encodes claims in a signed/encrypted token (header.payload.signature) - use access tokens (short-lived) and refresh tokens (long-lived, stored securely). Session management: server-side sessions (Redis, database) with cookie-based session IDs. MFA (Multi-Factor Authentication): TOTP (Google Authenticator, Authy), SMS codes, hardware keys (WebAuthn, FIDO2 U2F, YubiKey). Passkeys are platform authenticator-based credentials (WebAuthn) with sync across devices. WebAuthn enables passwordless authentication with public key cryptography - biometric or PIN protected on device.

authorization.content: Authorization controls what authenticated users can do. RBAC (Role-Based Access Control) assigns permissions to roles, roles to users - simple and widely supported but can become rigid. ABAC (Attribute-Based Access Control) evaluates policies against attributes (user, resource, environment) - flexible but complex to manage. ReBAC (Relationship-Based Access Control) bases decisions on relationships between entities (Google Zanzibar - /users/:id/friends/:friend_id). OPA (Open Policy Agent) provides policy-as-code with Rego language, decoupled from application logic - used with Kubernetes, Envoy, and API gateways. Casbin provides access control libraries in multiple languages with RBAC, ABAC, and custom models. Permission systems design: define resources, actions (CRUD, custom), and effects (allow/deny). ACLs (Access Control Lists) directly associate users with permissions on specific objects. Best practices: deny by default, least privilege, separation of duties, regular audits, and centralized policy management. Framework integrations: Spring Security, ASP.NET Core Authorization, Django Guardian, CanCanCan (Rails).

caching.content: Caching improves performance by storing frequently accessed data. CDN caching (Cloudflare, Akamai, Fastly) caches static assets (images, CSS, JS) at edge locations. Redis provides in-memory data structure store with eviction policies (LRU, LFU, TTL), persistence (RDB/AOF), and pub/sub. Memcached is a simpler distributed memory object caching system. HTTP caching: Cache-Control headers (max-age, no-cache, no-store, must-revalidate), ETag for conditional requests, Expires headers. Browser caching stores resources in disk/memory cache with cache busting via content hashes in filenames. Write strategies: write-through (write to cache and database simultaneously, consistent but slower writes), write-around (write to database only, cache populated on read, avoids cache pollution), write-behind (write to cache first, asynchronously write to database, fast but risk of data loss). Cache invalidation is one of the two hard things in computer science: TTL-based, event-driven (publish invalidation events), or version-based. Cache warming pre-populates cache with expected hot data on startup.

message_queues.content: Message queues enable asynchronous, decoupled communication between services. Apache Kafka is a distributed event streaming platform with topics, partitions, consumer groups, and exactly-once semantics. Key concepts: log-based storage (append-only), offset-based consumption, partitioning for parallelism, replication for durability. RabbitMQ implements AMQP with exchanges (direct, topic, fanout, headers), queues, bindings, and acknowledgements - supports priority queues, dead letter exchanges, and delayed messaging. Amazon SQS is fully managed with standard (at-least-once, high throughput) and FIFO (exactly-once, ordered) queues. NATS is lightweight, high-performance messaging (at-most-once, at-least-once, exactly-once via JetStream). Apache Pulsar combines streaming (Kafka-like) and queuing (RabbitMQ-like) with tiered storage. Ordering: Kafka guarantees per-partition ordering, RabbitMQ per-queue ordering, SQS FIFO per-message-group ordering. Exactly-once semantics combine idempotent producers, transactions, and idempotent consumers. Consumer patterns: competing consumers (work queues), broadcast (pub/sub), and routing (directed messages). Dead letter queues capture failed messages for later analysis.

real_time.content: Real-time communication enables instant data delivery. WebSocket provides full-duplex communication over a single TCP connection (ws://, wss://) with standardized protocol (RFC 6455) and binary/text frames. Server-Sent Events (SSE) provides unidirectional server-to-client streaming over HTTP with automatic reconnection and event IDs. WebRTC enables peer-to-peer real-time communication for audio, video, and data channels with NAT traversal (STUN/TURN/ICE). Polling strategies: short polling (periodic requests, simple but inefficient), long polling (hold request open until data available, then complete and re-request). Socket.IO (Node.js) provides WebSocket with fallback to HTTP long polling, rooms, namespaces, and automatic reconnection. Action Cable (Rails) integrates WebSockets with Rails app using Redis adapter. Phoenix Channels (Elixir) provides fast WebSocket with presence tracking. Key considerations: connection management (heartbeat/ping-pong), reconnection strategy (exponential backoff), message ordering, backpressure, broadcast efficiency (fan-out vs selective), and horizontal scaling (shared pub/sub like Redis).

search.content: Search engines provide full-text search, faceted navigation, and relevance ranking. Elasticsearch is the most popular distributed search engine based on Lucene - JSON documents, inverted indexes, tokenization/analysis pipeline, and query DSL. Key concepts: mapping (schema definition), analyzers (standard, custom with tokenizer + filters), scoring (TF-IDF, BM25), aggregations (buckets, metrics, pipeline). Typesense is an open-source, typo-tolerant search engine designed for speed (sub-50ms) with instant search, faceting, and geosearch. Meilisearch provides an out-of-the-box solution with search-as-you-type, typo tolerance, and minimal configuration. Algolia is a hosted search API with instant search, analytics, A/B testing, and AI-powered relevance. Solr is the older Lucene-based enterprise search platform with faceting, clustering, and rich document handling. Indexing strategy: decide fields to index (full-text, exact match, numeric, geo), analyzer configuration (language-specific stemming, synonyms, stop words), and refresh interval (near-real-time vs batch). Relevance tuning: boost fields (title > description > body), function scoring (recency, popularity), and query-time boosting.

file_storage.content: File storage solutions handle upload, serving, and management of user-generated content. Amazon S3 is the industry standard object store with bucket policies, versioning, lifecycle rules, and event notifications. Google Cloud Storage offers similar capabilities with uniform/bucket-level ACLs and object lifecycle management. Azure Blob Storage provides hot, cool, and archive access tiers. MinIO is an open-source S3-compatible object store for on-premises deployment. Local storage (filesystem) is suitable for development but not production (scaling, backup, migration). CDN integration (CloudFront, Cloud CDN, Fastly) serves cached content from edge locations. Presigned URLs grant temporary access to private objects (GET for downloads, PUT for uploads) without exposing credentials. Upload strategies: direct-to-S3 presigned POST, multipart upload (large files), chunked upload with resumable capability (tus protocol). Image optimization: server-side resizing (Sharp, ImageMagick), on-the-fly transformation (Imgix, Cloudinary), WebP/AVIF conversion. Security: input validation (file type, size, content scanning), access control per object, encryption at rest (SSE-S3, SSE-C, SSE-KMS), and secure transfer (HTTPS).

email.content: Email delivery services handle transactional and marketing emails. SMTP is the underlying protocol - direct sending (port 25, frequently blocked) or through services. SendGrid provides deliverability, analytics, templates (dynamic with Handlebars), and SMTP/API. Amazon SES offers low-cost sending with high deliverability and integration with SNS/S3. Mailgun provides email validation, routing, and analytics. DKIM (DomainKeys Identified Mail) adds digital signature to emails for authentication. SPF (Sender Policy Framework) specifies authorized sending IPs via DNS TXT records. DMARC (Domain-based Message Authentication, Reporting & Conformance) tells receivers how to handle unauthenticated email (none, quarantine, reject). Email templates: HTML with inline CSS (email client support varies), responsive design (MJML framework simplifies), tested across clients (Litmus, Email on Acid). Transactional vs marketing: transactional (password resets, receipts, notifications - must deliver promptly), marketing (newsletters, promotions - rate-limited, unsubscribe required per CAN-SPAM). Webhook handling: track opens (pixel), clicks, bounces, complaints, and unsubscribes. Email validation: verify format, domain existence, MX records, and mailbox existence.

payment.content: Payment processing handles financial transactions securely. Stripe is the developer-friendly choice with Payment Intents API, Checkout (hosted page), Elements (embedded UI), and Stripe.js for PCI compliance. PayPal provides hosted checkout, PayPal Checkout API, and Braintree (also owned by PayPal, supports multiple gateways). Square offers POS integration, e-commerce APIs, and Reader SDK for in-person payments. Braintree (by PayPal) supports multiple payment methods (cards, PayPal, Venmo, Apple Pay, Google Pay) with vault for stored credentials. Subscription management: Stripe Billing (recurring, metered, tiered pricing), Recurly, Chargebee. Webhook handling: process asynchronous events (payment succeeded, failed, dispute created) reliably with idempotency keys, secret verification, and retry logic. PCI Compliance: Level 1 (SAQ A - never handle card data, use Stripe Elements/Checkout), Level 2-4 (more requirements). SAQ A is simplest - outsource card data handling. Refund handling: full, partial, with reason tracking. Dispute resolution: gather evidence (receipt, proof of delivery, customer communication), respond within timeframe. Multi-currency support depends on gateway capabilities and currency conversion.

maps.content: Mapping services provide geographic visualization, geocoding, and routing. Google Maps Platform offers Maps JavaScript API (interactive maps), Geocoding API (address to coordinates), Directions API (route calculation), Places API (POI search), and Street View. Mapbox provides customizable vector tiles, GL JS (WebGL-powered maps), Navigation SDK, and Studio for style design. Leaflet is an open-source JavaScript library with plugins (clustering, heatmaps, drawing) and various tile providers. OpenStreetMap (OSM) is the community-driven map data source used by Leaflet and many other services. Geocoding converts addresses to coordinates (forward) or coordinates to addresses (reverse). Routing considers traffic, transit, walking, cycling, and multiple waypoints. Tiles: raster tiles (pre-rendered images) vs vector tiles (rendered client-side, smaller, customizable, interactive). Clustering groups nearby markers at lower zoom levels (Leaflet.markercluster, Supercluster). Heatmaps show data density distribution. Performance: limit visible markers, use GeoJSON for data transport, implement spatial filtering (bounding box queries). Offline maps: tile caching, Mapbox offline SDK, Organic Maps.

push_notifications.content: Push notifications deliver time-sensitive information to users. APNs (Apple Push Notification service) delivers to iOS, macOS, watchOS, tvOS - uses TLS with token-based or certificate-based authentication. FCM (Firebase Cloud Messaging) delivers to Android, iOS (via APNs proxy), and web browsers. Web Push Protocol (W3C standard) delivers to browsers supporting Service Workers with VAPID for server identification. Notification channels (Android) and categories (iOS) let users granularly control notification types. Delivery guarantees: APNs/FCM provide best-effort delivery - don't rely on guaranteed delivery. Priority: high (immediate display), normal (batched, battery-efficient). Batching: coalesce multiple notifications into a summary. Payload limits: APNs 4KB, FCM 4KB (Android) / 2KB (iOS). Local notifications are scheduled on-device, no network required. Key patterns: register device tokens on app launch, handle token refresh, manage topic subscriptions (FCM topics/channel groups), implement notification grouping (Android Notification Groups, iOS threadIdentifier). Analytics: delivery rate, open rate, conversion tracking. Permission handling: request at appropriate time (not on first launch), handle denied/rejected gracefully.

analytics.content: Product analytics track user behavior and product performance. Google Analytics 4 (GA4) is the standard for web analytics with event-based data model, user properties, and predictive metrics. Mixpanel focuses on user behavior analytics with event tracking, funnels, retention, and A/B testing. Amplitude offers product analytics with behavioral cohorts, personas, and experiment platform. PostHog is open-source product analytics with session recording, feature flags, and heatmaps. Plausible is privacy-focused, lightweight web analytics (GDPR compliant, no cookies). Event tracking: standardized naming conventions (snake_case, Object.Action format), properties (user_id, timestamp, session_id, source, medium), and event properties. Funnels track step-by-step conversion. Retention cohorts show returning users over time. User identification: anonymous (device ID), identified (user ID post-login), with merging via identify calls. Data pipeline: client SDK -> server SDK -> batch processing -> data warehouse (BigQuery, Snowflake) -> visualization. Privacy: cookie consent (GDPR), data retention policies, IP anonymization, opt-out mechanisms. Key metrics: DAU/MAU, retention rate, conversion rate, churn rate, LTV (Lifetime Value), CAC (Customer Acquisition Cost).

logging.content: Logging provides system observability and debugging capability. ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) is the most popular log management solution: Logstash ingests and transforms logs, Elasticsearch indexes and stores, Kibana visualizes. Loki (Grafana Labs) is a horizontally-scalable, highly-available log aggregation system inspired by Prometheus - indexes labels, not log content. Datadog provides integrated logs, metrics, and traces in a single platform with log-based metrics and log pipelines. Structured logging outputs logs as JSON (not plain text) with consistent fields: timestamp, level, logger, message, context (request_id, user_id, correlation_id), error (type, message, stack_trace). Log levels: TRACE (detailed debug), DEBUG (development), INFO (normal operation), WARN (potential issue), ERROR (recoverable error), FATAL (unrecoverable). Correlation IDs propagate through distributed systems (generated at entry point, passed via HTTP headers) to trace requests across services. Distributed tracing (OpenTelemetry, Jaeger, Zipkin) records spans with parent-child relationships across service boundaries. Best practices: log at appropriate level, include actionable context, avoid sensitive data (PII, credentials), use async logging to avoid performance impact, manage log rotation and retention.

monitoring.content: Monitoring ensures system reliability and performance. Prometheus is the leading open-source monitoring and alerting toolkit with multi-dimensional data model (time series identified by metric name and key/value labels), PromQL query language, and pull-based collection. Grafana provides visualization, alerting, and dashboards with support for Prometheus, Loki, Tempo, and 100+ data sources. Datadog is a SaaS monitoring platform with infrastructure, APM, logs, and synthetic monitoring. New Relic offers application performance monitoring (APM) with distributed tracing, browser monitoring, and mobile monitoring. RED metrics (Rate, Errors, Duration) are the standard for service monitoring. USE metrics (Utilization, Saturation, Errors) for infrastructure resources. SLOs (Service Level Objectives) set target reliability (99.9% uptime). SLIs (Service Level Indicators) measure actual performance (latency p99, error rate, throughput). Alerting: define alert rules with severity, notification channels (PagerDuty, Slack, email), escalation policies, and runbooks. Burn rate alerts trigger when error budget is consumed faster than expected. Best practices: dashboard per service/system, four golden signals (latency, traffic, errors, saturation), threshold-based and anomaly detection alerts, and on-call rotation with incident response process.

crash_reporting.content: Crash reporting captures and analyzes application errors. Sentry is the leading error tracking platform supporting 40+ languages and frameworks with automatic error grouping, stack trace unwinding, breadcrumbs, and release tracking. Crashlytics (Firebase) provides real-time crash reporting for mobile apps with analytics integration and cohort analysis. Bugsnag offers error monitoring with automatic breadcrumb collection, session tracking, and diagnostic data. Symbolication maps minified/obfuscated stack traces back to original source code - upload source maps (JS), dSYM files (iOS), Breakpad symbols (native). Breadcrumbs track user actions leading up to a crash (navigation, API calls, UI interactions). Release health tracks adoption, crash-free rate, and issue frequency per version. Key metrics: crash-free rate (percentage of sessions without crash), crash rate (crashes per user/device), impact (affected users). Best practices: set appropriate logging level, filter/ignore known non-actionable errors (network timeouts, third-party errors), group issues by fingerprint (not just stack trace), set up alerting for new regressions, and include relevant context (user ID, device info, app state). Privacy: respect user opt-out, avoid capturing PII in breadcrumbs and extra data.

a_b_testing.content: A/B testing compares variants to determine which performs better. Feature flags (LaunchDarkly, Flagsmith, Unleash) enable conditional feature activation without deployment - kill switches, gradual rollouts, targeting by user attributes. Experimentation platforms (Optimizely, Google Optimize, VWO) use statistical methods to determine winner: define hypothesis, set sample size, randomize assignment, measure metrics, and analyze results. Statistical significance (p-value < 0.05) indicates observed difference is likely not due to chance. Power analysis determines required sample size (depends on effect size, significance level, desired power 80%). Multi-armed bandit algorithms (epsilon-greedy, Thompson sampling) dynamically allocate traffic to better-performing variants, more efficient than fixed A/B tests. Key metrics: conversion rate, retention, revenue per user, and guardrail metrics (ensure no negative impact). Pitfalls: peeking (checking results early), Simpson's paradox (confounding variables), multiple comparison problem (Bonferroni correction). Infrastructure: split traffic at edge (Cloudflare Workers, Vercel Edge Config), server-side (cookies, user IDs), or client-side (JavaScript SDK). Sample ratio mismatch (SRM) check ensures traffic is evenly distributed.
